summary:
Abdominal ultrasound has no specific evidence supporting its routine use in diagnosing or managing constipated children; it is not a standard tool in their care despite being able to evaluate gastrointestinal anatomy. Its use should remain selective—only when red flags suggesting obstructive or structural conditions are present—and otherwise falls outside conventional practice.  

Final summary:

Abdominal ultrasound is not indicated for routine diagnosis or follow-up of constipated children due to lacking supportive evidence. It should be reserved solely for select cases presenting concerning signs such as suspected anatomical abnormality or bowel obstruction.
